ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product class; no specific braised eggplant dish class exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked aubergine/eggplant as the closest defensible category; no braised-dish-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for braised eggplant dish identified; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of braised eggplant as served in a Singaporean Chinese restaurant or hawker stall is approximately 400 g, typically shared as a side dish alongside rice or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11210); adapted for braised preparation with soy sauce, oil, and aromatics
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant values used as the base. Macros adjusted upward to reflect added oil (~8 g fat/100 g), soy sauce sodium (~300 mg/100 g), oyster sauce sugar contribution (~3 g/100 g), and cornstarch carbohydrate (~8.75 g/100 g total). Energy recalculated at 4-4-9 kcal/g. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database entries for Chinese braised vegetable dishes. Input record values retained as they are nutritionally coherent with this adapted profile.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. — Eggplant GI value (GI ≈ 15, low); University of Sydney GI Database entry for eggplant.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggplant has a published GI of approximately 15 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care). Braising with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and cornstarch adds modest carbohydrate; the overall dish GI is estimated at 15–20 (low), conservatively set at 15. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 35 g) / 100 = 5.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per 400 g serving) approaches the upper daily limit for hypertensive individuals; soy sauce and oyster sauce are the primary contributors.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce when ordering, limit portion size to 150–200 g, and avoid adding extra soy sauce at the table; the dish's eggplant-derived potassium (~220 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) provide partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Eggplant is a low-GI vegetable (GI ≈ 15); the dish has low sugar (3 g/100 g) and moderate dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) which together support attenuated postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised eggplant is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (150–200 g); request lighter cornstarch thickening and reduced oyster sauce to minimise added sugar, and pair with low-GI staples such as brown rice or wholegrain noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.25 g/100 g and cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g; however, sodium at 300 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern at full serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish's low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile are favourable for cardiovascular health, but individuals with CVD should limit sodium intake by requesting sauce reduction and keeping the serving to 150–200 g to stay within a safe daily sodium budget.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in solanine-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine) should avoid this dish entirely; ginger and garlic in the recipe provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols but are insufficient to offset the nightshade concern for sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Eggplant (红烧茄子, Hóng Shāo Qié Zi) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and hawker stalls. Tender eggplant (aubergine) is first pan-fried or stir-fried in vegetable oil until softened and lightly caramelised, then braised in a savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, a touch of sugar, and sometimes vinegar. Cornstarch is used to thicken the sauce to a glossy, clingy consistency. The finished dish has a silky, melt-in-the-mouth texture with a deep umami flavour balanced by subtle sweetness and a mild tang. It is typically served as a shared side dish alongside steamed white rice or noodles. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 120 kcal, 2 g protein, 8.75 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil, with only 1.25 g saturated fat), 8.75 g carbohydrate, 3 g sugar, 2 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The eggplant base contributes potassium (~220 mg/100 g), iron (0.38 mg/100 g), and a small amount of vitamin C (1.25 mg/100 g). The dish is low-GI (GI ≈ 15) and suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. Sodium content is the primary concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients, and the dish should be avoided by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables. It is vegetarian and dairy-free but contains soy and gluten from soy sauce and oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant data with adjustments for added oil, soy sauce sodium, and oyster sauce sugar contributions. The input record values are nutritionally coherent with this adapted profile and have been retained. No exact HPB Singapore entry for this specific braised preparation was available; the adaptation is well-documented and cross-referenced. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this dish as prepared.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this braised eggplant preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; the fat is predominantly from vegetable cooking oil but the specific fatty acid split is not reported for this dish type. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this braised preparation is not reported in the adapted source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific braised preparation in the adapted approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for a plant-based dish cooked in vegetable oil with no animal fat; consistent with USDA data for cooked eggplant and vegetable oil. potassium_mg value of 220.0 mg/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant (~123 mg/100 g base) plus contribution from green onions and garlic; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct analysis. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to oyster sauce. Halal status assumes halal-certified soy sauce and oyster sauce are used, as is standard in Singapore hawker preparation. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not constitute an approved primary evidence source under Project Veritas v2 policy; USDA FoodData Central was used instead.
